text.title: Performance Architecture Core Rules
text.summary: 7 testable rules grounding the performance-architecture methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check with sourced rationale; the final rule is the skip-this-methodology gate referenced by the decision tree.

rule.statement: SLO targets MUST be numeric (p50 / p95 / p99 in ms; availability in %; error budget in %). Qualitative SLOs ('fast', 'snappy') MUST be rejected.
rule.rationale: Numeric targets are testable; qualitative targets cannot be load-tested and become subjective.

rule.statement: Performance work MUST start from a baseline measurement (k6 / vegeta report). Optimisations MUST cite which percentile improves and by how much.

rationale.source: Brendan Gregg — Systems Performance; perf-regression postmortems.

rule.rationale: Optimisation without baseline is intuition-driven; 'rocket science' fixes often hurt the percentile that mattered.

rule.statement: Cache layers MUST be applied edge-inward: CDN first for static + GET-cacheable, application cache (Redis) for hot reads, DB-level cache last. Skipping a layer to cache deeper requires explicit justification.

rationale.source: CDN provider whitepapers; performance audit playbooks.

rule.rationale: Edge cache shaves latency + cost the most; in-layer caching shifts but does not remove load.

rule.statement: The load-test gate MUST encode the SLO as thresholds (e.g. k6 thresholds, vegeta success rate). A test that passes while the SLO is missed is a defective test.
rule.rationale: SLO and load-test are the same contract; divergence is a latent regression source.

rule.statement: Spec MUST name observable scaling triggers (CPU%, queue depth, p95 latency growth) with thresholds; auto-scaling configs MUST match the triggers.
rule.rationale: Scaling triggered by gut produces flapping; named observable triggers tie capacity to user-visible signals.

rule.statement: When prerequisites hold (SLOs, baseline, top-3 endpoints, cache topology), apply r1 → r2 → r3 → r4 → r5 and emit the spec.
rule.rationale: Order encodes risk: numeric SLO first, baseline second, cache plan third, gate fourth, scaling fifth.

rule.statement: Do NOT use when (a) no latency contract exists, (b) requirements unstable, (c) headroom already > 50% and SLOs met. Note constraint and exit.
rule.rationale: Spec ceremony in these contexts wastes review time.
